[[ja:鬼の窟古墳 (西都市)]] [[ja:鬼の窟古墳]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito)}} {{wikidata link|Q125960571}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:History of Saito, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Kofun of Miyazaki prefecture]] [[Category:National historic sites in Miyazaki prefecture]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito)]][[ja:鬼の窟古墳 (西都市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito)]][[ja:鬼の窟古墳 (西都市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito)]][[ja:鬼の窟古墳 (西都市)]] {{Infobox ancient site}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q125960571]] Wikidata name: Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Oni no Iwaya Kofun''' (鬼の窟古墳) or '''Saitobaru No. 206 Kofun''' (西都原206号墳) is a {{ill|circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/circular Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} located in Miyake, [[:en:Saito, Miyazaki|Saito]], [[:en:Miyazaki Prefecture|Miyazaki Prefecture]], Japan. It is part of the [[:en:Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]], designated as a [[Special Historic Site of Japan]].
== Overview == [[:en:Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]] is located on a plateau along the southern bank of the Hitotsuse River in central Miyazaki Prefecture. This particular kofun was constructed independently at the center of the cluster. Its name derives from a local legend where a demon, infatuated with {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}}, built the stone chamber overnight. <ref name="説明板">Historical site information board.</ref>
The kofun has been known since the Meiji period, with its first documentation by [[:en:William Gowland|William Gowland]]. It underwent detailed surveys in 1984 and 1992 and preservation work in 1995.
The mound is circular, measuring 36.4 meters east-west, 33.6 meters north-south, and 7.3 meters high. It has a two-tiered structure with no evidence of [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] on its surface. Surrounding the mound are an outer embankment and double moats, with the inner moat (内壕) 9.9-11 meters wide, the embankment 5.2 meters high, and the outer moat (外壕) approximately 5 meters wide.
The burial chamber is a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, accessible from the south, with an overall length of 12.5 meters. The chamber's construction employs massive sandstone blocks. Artifacts recovered include earrings, jade, iron arrowheads, gilt-bronze harness fittings, and pottery ([[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]]), indicating its construction in the late 6th to early 7th century. Adjacent to the southwest is No. 205 Kofun, believed to be a satellite mound due to overlapping pottery finds.
The kofun is considered the last key burial of a regional leader immediately following the cessation of [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] construction in Saitobaru. Its similarities with the [[:en:Ishibutai Kofun|Ishibutai Kofun]] in Nara Prefecture suggest close ties with the Yamato court.
== Excavation History == * 1823 (Bunsei 6): Kodama Sanemitsu documented the chamber in "Hyuga no Kuni Kamiyo Ezu." <ref>{{Harvid|宮崎県史 資料編 考古2|1993}}</ref> * 1872-1888 (Meiji 5-21): William Gowland created schematic diagrams and introduced the kofun in his work on Japanese dolmens. * 1934 (Showa 9): Designated a [[National Historic Site of Japan]] as part of the "Saitobaru Kofun Cluster." * 1952 (Showa 27): Elevated to a [[:en:Special Historic Site|Special Historic Site]]. * 1984 (Showa 59): Survey and chamber measurements. * 1995 (Heisei 7): Excavation and preservation work. * Reconstruction work restored the chamber's forecourt and part of its passage.
== Burial Chamber == The chamber measures: * Total length: 12.4 meters * Main chamber: Length 4.9 meters, width 1.75–2.45 meters, height 2.15 meters * Passage: Length 7.5 meters, width 1.8 meters, height 1.5 meters
Sandstone boulders form the chamber's structure, with a slightly rectangular shape that widens toward the entrance. Stones are layered in four tiers with slanted walls. The floor is paved with river stones. The chamber's roofing consists of three large slabs, while the passage also features river stone paving and three ceiling stones.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (toward rear wall) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (toward entrance) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), sendou-2.jpg|Passage (toward entrance) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), funkyu-3.jpg|Mound and chamber entrance </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Saitobaru Kofun Cluster''' (Special Historic Site since 1952) * Artifacts are displayed at the {{ill|Miyazaki Prefectural Saitobaru Archaeological Museum|ja|宮崎県立西都原考古博物館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Prefectural Saitobaru Archaeological Museum|qq=}}.

== Further Reading == * Miyazaki Prefecture History (Archaeology Section, Volume 2), Miyazaki Prefecture, 1993. * "Saitobaru Kofun Cluster Excavation Report," Miyazaki Prefectural Board of Education, 2000.

It is part of the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|en|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|WD=Q3469314|lt=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster}}, designated as a [[Special Historic Site of Japan]].
== Overview == {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|en|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|WD=Q3469314|lt=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster}} is located on a plateau along the southern bank of the Hitotsuse River in central Miyazaki Prefecture. This particular kofun was constructed independently at the center of the cluster. Its name derives from a local legend where a demon, infatuated with {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}}, built the stone chamber overnight. <ref name="説明板">Historical site information board.</ref>
The kofun has been known since the Meiji period, with its first documentation by {{ill|William Gowland|en|William Gowland|WD=Q3568666|lt=William Gowland}}. It underwent detailed surveys in 1984 and 1992 and preservation work in 1995.
The mound is circular, measuring 36.4 meters east-west, 33.6 meters north-south, and 7.3 meters high. It has a two-tiered structure with no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|en|Fukiishi|WD=Q11621213|lt=fukiishi}} on its surface. Surrounding the mound are an outer embankment and double moats, with the inner moat (内壕) 9.9-11 meters wide, the embankment 5.2 meters high, and the outer moat (外壕) approximately 5 meters wide.
The burial chamber is a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, accessible from the south, with an overall length of 12.5 meters. The chamber's construction employs massive sandstone blocks. Artifacts recovered include earrings, jade, iron arrowheads, gilt-bronze harness fittings, and pottery ({{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}}), indicating its construction in the late 6th to early 7th century. Adjacent to the southwest is No. 205 Kofun, believed to be a satellite mound due to overlapping pottery finds.
The kofun is considered the last key burial of a regional leader immediately following the cessation of {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} construction in Saitobaru. Its similarities with the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|en|Ishibutai Kofun|WD=Q3816159|lt=Ishibutai Kofun}} in Nara Prefecture suggest close ties with the Yamato court.
== Excavation History == * 1823 (Bunsei 6): Kodama Sanemitsu documented the chamber in "Hyuga no Kuni Kamiyo Ezu." <ref>{{Harvid|宮崎県史 資料編 考古2|1993}}</ref> * 1872-1888 (Meiji 5-21): William Gowland created schematic diagrams and introduced the kofun in his work on Japanese dolmens. * 1934 (Showa 9): Designated a [[National Historic Site of Japan]] as part of the "Saitobaru Kofun Cluster." * 1952 (Showa 27): Elevated to a {{ill|Special Historic Site|en|Special Historic Site|WD=Q26764449|lt=Special Historic Site}}. * 1984 (Showa 59): Survey and chamber measurements. * 1995 (Heisei 7): Excavation and preservation work. * Reconstruction work restored the chamber's forecourt and part of its passage.
== Burial Chamber == The chamber measures: * Total length: 12.4 meters * Main chamber: Length 4.9 meters, width 1.75–2.45 meters, height 2.15 meters * Passage: Length 7.5 meters, width 1.8 meters, height 1.5 meters
Sandstone boulders form the chamber's structure, with a slightly rectangular shape that widens toward the entrance. Stones are layered in four tiers with slanted walls. The floor is paved with river stones. The chamber's roofing consists of three large slabs, while the passage also features river stone paving and three ceiling stones.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (toward rear wall) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (toward entrance) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), sendou-2.jpg|Passage (toward entrance) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), funkyu-3.jpg|Mound and chamber entrance </gallery>

== Overview == {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|en|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|WD=Q3469314|lt=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster}} is located on a plateau along the southern bank of the Hitotsuse River in central Miyazaki Prefecture. This particular kofun was constructed independently at the center of the cluster. Its name derives from a local legend where a demon, infatuated with {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}}, built the stone chamber overnight. <ref name="説明板">Historical site information board.</ref>
The kofun has been known since the Meiji period, with its first documentation by {{ill|William Gowland|en|William Gowland|WD=Q3568666|lt=William Gowland}}. It underwent detailed surveys in 1984 and 1992 and preservation work in 1995.
The mound is circular, measuring 36.4 meters east-west, 33.6 meters north-south, and 7.3 meters high. It has a two-tiered structure with no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|en|Fukiishi|WD=Q11621213|lt=fukiishi}} on its surface. Surrounding the mound are an outer embankment and double moats, with the inner moat (内壕) 9.9-11 meters wide, the embankment 5.2 meters high, and the outer moat (外壕) approximately 5 meters wide.
The burial chamber is a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, accessible from the south, with an overall length of 12.5 meters. The chamber's construction employs massive sandstone blocks. Artifacts recovered include earrings, jade, iron arrowheads, gilt-bronze harness fittings, and pottery ({{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}}), indicating its construction in the late 6th to early 7th century. Adjacent to the southwest is No. 205 Kofun, believed to be a satellite mound due to overlapping pottery finds.
The kofun is considered the last key burial of a regional leader immediately following the cessation of {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} construction in Saitobaru. Its similarities with the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|en|Ishibutai Kofun|WD=Q3816159|lt=Ishibutai Kofun}} in Nara Prefecture suggest close ties with the Yamato court.
== Excavation History == * 1823 (Bunsei 6): Kodama Sanemitsu documented the chamber in "Hyuga no Kuni Kamiyo Ezu." <ref>{{Harvid|宮崎県史 資料編 考古2|1993}}</ref> * 1872-1888 (Meiji 5-21): William Gowland created schematic diagrams and introduced the kofun in his work on Japanese dolmens. * 1934 (Showa 9): Designated a [[National Historic Site of Japan]] as part of the "Saitobaru Kofun Cluster." * 1952 (Showa 27): Elevated to a {{ill|Special Historic Site|en|Special Historic Site|WD=Q26764449|lt=Special Historic Site}}. * 1984 (Showa 59): Survey and chamber measurements. * 1995 (Heisei 7): Excavation and preservation work. * Reconstruction work restored the chamber's forecourt and part of its passage.
== Burial Chamber == The chamber measures: * Total length: 12.4 meters * Main chamber: Length 4.9 meters, width 1.75–2.45 meters, height 2.15 meters * Passage: Length 7.5 meters, width 1.8 meters, height 1.5 meters
Sandstone boulders form the chamber's structure, with a slightly rectangular shape that widens toward the entrance. Stones are layered in four tiers with slanted walls. The floor is paved with river stones. The chamber's roofing consists of three large slabs, while the passage also features river stone paving and three ceiling stones.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (toward rear wall) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (toward entrance) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), sendou-2.jpg|Passage (toward entrance) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), funkyu-3.jpg|Mound and chamber entrance </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Saitobaru Kofun Cluster''' (Special Historic Site since 1952) * Artifacts are displayed at the {{ill|Miyazaki Prefectural Saitobaru Archaeological Museum|ja|宮崎県立西都原考古博物館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Prefectural Saitobaru Archaeological Museum|qq=}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Miyazaki Prefecture History (Archaeology Section, Volume 2), Miyazaki Prefecture, 1993. * "Saitobaru Kofun Cluster Excavation Report," Miyazaki Prefectural Board of Education, 2000.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|en|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|WD=Q3469314|lt=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster}} * {{ill|Jōshinzuka Kofun|en|Jōshinzuka Kofun|WD=Q11481383|lt=Jōshinzuka Kofun}}
== External Links == * {{National Heritage Database|401|2918|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster}}

The burial chamber is a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, accessible from the south, with an overall length of 12.5 meters. The chamber's construction employs massive sandstone blocks. Artifacts recovered include earrings, jade, iron arrowheads, gilt-bronze harness fittings, and pottery ({{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}}), indicating its construction in the late 6th to early 7th century. Adjacent to the southwest is No. 205 Kofun, believed to be a satellite mound due to overlapping pottery finds.
The kofun is considered the last key burial of a regional leader immediately following the cessation of {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} construction in Saitobaru. Its similarities with the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|en|Ishibutai Kofun|WD=Q3816159|lt=Ishibutai Kofun}} in Nara Prefecture suggest close ties with the Yamato court.
== Excavation History == * 1823 (Bunsei 6): Kodama Sanemitsu documented the chamber in "Hyuga no Kuni Kamiyo Ezu." <ref>{{Harvid|宮崎県史 資料編 考古2|1993}}</ref> * 1872-1888 (Meiji 5-21): William Gowland created schematic diagrams and introduced the kofun in his work on Japanese dolmens. * 1934 (Showa 9): Designated a [[National Historic Site of Japan]] as part of the "Saitobaru Kofun Cluster." * 1952 (Showa 27): Elevated to a {{ill|Special Historic Site|en|Special Historic Site|WD=Q26764449|lt=Special Historic Site}}. * 1984 (Showa 59): Survey and chamber measurements. * 1995 (Heisei 7): Excavation and preservation work. * Reconstruction work restored the chamber's forecourt and part of its passage.
== Burial Chamber == The chamber measures: * Total length: 12.4 meters * Main chamber: Length 4.9 meters, width 1.75–2.45 meters, height 2.15 meters * Passage: Length 7.5 meters, width 1.8 meters, height 1.5 meters
Sandstone boulders form the chamber's structure, with a slightly rectangular shape that widens toward the entrance. Stones are layered in four tiers with slanted walls. The floor is paved with river stones. The chamber's roofing consists of three large slabs, while the passage also features river stone paving and three ceiling stones.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (toward rear wall) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (toward entrance) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), sendou-2.jpg|Passage (toward entrance) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), funkyu-3.jpg|Mound and chamber entrance </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Saitobaru Kofun Cluster''' (Special Historic Site since 1952) * Artifacts are displayed at the {{ill|Miyazaki Prefectural Saitobaru Archaeological Museum|ja|宮崎県立西都原考古博物館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Prefectural Saitobaru Archaeological Museum|qq=}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito)]][[ja:鬼の窟古墳 (西都市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito)]][[ja:鬼の窟古墳 (西都市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito)]][[ja:鬼の窟古墳 (西都市)]] {{Infobox ancient site}} '''Oni no Iwaya Kofun''' (鬼の窟古墳) or '''Saitobaru No. 206 Kofun''' (西都原206号墳) is a {{ill|circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/circular Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} located in Miyake, {{ill|Saito, Miyazaki|en|Saito, Miyazaki|WD=Q850479|lt=Saito|1=Saito, Miyazaki}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|en|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|WD=Q3469314|lt=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|1=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster}}, designated as a [[Special Historic Site of Japan]].
== Overview == {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|en|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|WD=Q3469314|lt=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|1=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster}} is located on a plateau along the southern bank of the Hitotsuse River in central Miyazaki Prefecture. This particular kofun was constructed independently at the center of the cluster. Its name derives from a local legend where a demon, infatuated with {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}}, built the stone chamber overnight. <ref name="説明板">Historical site information board.</ref>
The kofun has been known since the Meiji period, with its first documentation by {{ill|William Gowland|en|William Gowland|WD=Q3568666|lt=William Gowland|1=William Gowland}}. It underwent detailed surveys in 1984 and 1992 and preservation work in 1995.
The mound is circular, measuring 36.4 meters east-west, 33.6 meters north-south, and 7.3 meters high. It has a two-tiered structure with no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|en|Fukiishi|WD=Q11621213|lt=fukiishi|1=Fukiishi}} on its surface. Surrounding the mound are an outer embankment and double moats, with the inner moat (内壕) 9.9-11 meters wide, the embankment 5.2 meters high, and the outer moat (外壕) approximately 5 meters wide.
The burial chamber is a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, accessible from the south, with an overall length of 12.5 meters. The chamber's construction employs massive sandstone blocks. Artifacts recovered include earrings, jade, iron arrowheads, gilt-bronze harness fittings, and pottery ({{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}}), indicating its construction in the late 6th to early 7th century. Adjacent to the southwest is No. 205 Kofun, believed to be a satellite mound due to overlapping pottery finds.
The kofun is considered the last key burial of a regional leader immediately following the cessation of {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} construction in Saitobaru. Its similarities with the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|en|Ishibutai Kofun|WD=Q3816159|lt=Ishibutai Kofun|1=Ishibutai Kofun}} in Nara Prefecture suggest close ties with the Yamato court.
== Excavation History == * 1823 (Bunsei 6): Kodama Sanemitsu documented the chamber in "Hyuga no Kuni Kamiyo Ezu." <ref>{{Harvid|宮崎県史 資料編 考古2|1993}}</ref> * 1872-1888 (Meiji 5-21): William Gowland created schematic diagrams and introduced the kofun in his work on Japanese dolmens. * 1934 (Showa 9): Designated a [[National Historic Site of Japan]] as part of the "Saitobaru Kofun Cluster." * 1952 (Showa 27): Elevated to a {{ill|Special Historic Site|en|Special Historic Site|WD=Q26764449|lt=Special Historic Site|1=Special Historic Site}}. * 1984 (Showa 59): Survey and chamber measurements. * 1995 (Heisei 7): Excavation and preservation work. * Reconstruction work restored the chamber's forecourt and part of its passage.
== Burial Chamber == The chamber measures: * Total length: 12.4 meters * Main chamber: Length 4.9 meters, width 1.75–2.45 meters, height 2.15 meters * Passage: Length 7.5 meters, width 1.8 meters, height 1.5 meters
Sandstone boulders form the chamber's structure, with a slightly rectangular shape that widens toward the entrance. Stones are layered in four tiers with slanted walls. The floor is paved with river stones. The chamber's roofing consists of three large slabs, while the passage also features river stone paving and three ceiling stones.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (toward rear wall) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (toward entrance) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), sendou-2.jpg|Passage (toward entrance) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), funkyu-3.jpg|Mound and chamber entrance </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Saitobaru Kofun Cluster''' (Special Historic Site since 1952) * Artifacts are displayed at the {{ill|Miyazaki Prefectural Saitobaru Archaeological Museum|ja|宮崎県立西都原考古博物館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Prefectural Saitobaru Archaeological Museum|qq=}}.

== Further Reading == * Miyazaki Prefecture History (Archaeology Section, Volume 2), Miyazaki Prefecture, 1993. * "Saitobaru Kofun Cluster Excavation Report," Miyazaki Prefectural Board of Education, 2000.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|en|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|WD=Q3469314|lt=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|1=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster}} * {{ill|Jōshinzuka Kofun|en|Jōshinzuka Kofun|WD=Q11481383|lt=Jōshinzuka Kofun|1=Jōshinzuka Kofun}}

Its similarities with the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|en|Ishibutai Kofun|WD=Q3816159|lt=Ishibutai Kofun|1=Ishibutai Kofun}} in Nara Prefecture suggest close ties with the Yamato court.
== Excavation History == * 1823 (Bunsei 6): Kodama Sanemitsu documented the chamber in "Hyuga no Kuni Kamiyo Ezu." <ref>{{Harvid|宮崎県史 資料編 考古2|1993}}</ref> * 1872-1888 (Meiji 5-21): William Gowland created schematic diagrams and introduced the kofun in his work on Japanese dolmens. * 1934 (Showa 9): Designated a [[National Historic Site of Japan]] as part of the "Saitobaru Kofun Cluster." * 1952 (Showa 27): Elevated to a {{ill|Special Historic Site|en|Special Historic Site|WD=Q26764449|lt=Special Historic Site|1=Special Historic Site}}. * 1984 (Showa 59): Survey and chamber measurements. * 1995 (Heisei 7): Excavation and preservation work. * Reconstruction work restored the chamber's forecourt and part of its passage.
== Burial Chamber == The chamber measures: * Total length: 12.4 meters * Main chamber: Length 4.9 meters, width 1.75–2.45 meters, height 2.15 meters * Passage: Length 7.5 meters, width 1.8 meters, height 1.5 meters
Sandstone boulders form the chamber's structure, with a slightly rectangular shape that widens toward the entrance. Stones are layered in four tiers with slanted walls. The floor is paved with river stones. The chamber's roofing consists of three large slabs, while the passage also features river stone paving and three ceiling stones.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (toward rear wall) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (toward entrance) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), sendou-2.jpg|Passage (toward entrance) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), funkyu-3.jpg|Mound and chamber entrance </gallery>

[[ja:鬼の窟古墳 (西都市)]] [[ja:鬼の窟古墳]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito)}} {{wikidata link|Q125960571}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:History of Saito, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Kofun of Miyazaki prefecture]] [[Category:National historic sites in Miyazaki prefecture]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito)]][[ja:鬼の窟古墳 (西都市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito)]][[ja:鬼の窟古墳 (西都市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito)]][[ja:鬼の窟古墳 (西都市)]] {{Infobox ancient site}} '''Oni no Iwaya Kofun''' (鬼の窟古墳) or '''Saitobaru No. 206 Kofun''' (西都原206号墳) is a {{ill|circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/circular Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} located in Miyake, {{ill|Saito, Miyazaki|en|Saito, Miyazaki|WD=Q850479|lt=Saito|1=Saito, Miyazaki}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|en|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|WD=Q3469314|lt=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|1=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster}}, designated as a [[Special Historic Site of Japan]].
== Overview == {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|en|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|WD=Q3469314|lt=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|1=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster}} is located on a plateau along the southern bank of the Hitotsuse River in central Miyazaki Prefecture. This particular kofun was constructed independently at the center of the cluster. Its name derives from a local legend where a demon, infatuated with {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}}, built the stone chamber overnight. <ref name="説明板">Historical site information board.</ref>
The kofun has been known since the Meiji period, with its first documentation by {{ill|William Gowland|en|William Gowland|WD=Q3568666|lt=William Gowland|1=William Gowland}}. It underwent detailed surveys in 1984 and 1992 and preservation work in 1995.
The mound is circular, measuring 36.4 meters east-west, 33.6 meters north-south, and 7.3 meters high. It has a two-tiered structure with no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|en|Fukiishi|WD=Q11621213|lt=fukiishi|1=Fukiishi}} on its surface. Surrounding the mound are an outer embankment and double moats, with the inner moat (内壕) 9.9-11 meters wide, the embankment 5.2 meters high, and the outer moat (外壕) approximately 5 meters wide.
The burial chamber is a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, accessible from the south, with an overall length of 12.5 meters. The chamber's construction employs massive sandstone blocks. Artifacts recovered include earrings, jade, iron arrowheads, gilt-bronze harness fittings, and pottery ({{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}}), indicating its construction in the late 6th to early 7th century. Adjacent to the southwest is No. 205 Kofun, believed to be a satellite mound due to overlapping pottery finds.
The kofun is considered the last key burial of a regional leader immediately following the cessation of {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} construction in Saitobaru. Its similarities with the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|en|Ishibutai Kofun|WD=Q3816159|lt=Ishibutai Kofun|1=Ishibutai Kofun}} in Nara Prefecture suggest close ties with the Yamato court.
== Excavation History == * 1823 (Bunsei 6): Kodama Sanemitsu documented the chamber in "Hyuga no Kuni Kamiyo Ezu." <ref>{{Harvid|宮崎県史 資料編 考古2|1993}}</ref> * 1872-1888 (Meiji 5-21): William Gowland created schematic diagrams and introduced the kofun in his work on Japanese dolmens. * 1934 (Showa 9): Designated a [[National Historic Site of Japan]] as part of the "Saitobaru Kofun Cluster." * 1952 (Showa 27): Elevated to a {{ill|Special Historic Site|en|Special Historic Site|WD=Q26764449|lt=Special Historic Site|1=Special Historic Site}}. * 1984 (Showa 59): Survey and chamber measurements. * 1995 (Heisei 7): Excavation and preservation work. * Reconstruction work restored the chamber's forecourt and part of its passage.
== Burial Chamber == The chamber measures: * Total length: 12.4 meters * Main chamber: Length 4.9 meters, width 1.75–2.45 meters, height 2.15 meters * Passage: Length 7.5 meters, width 1.8 meters, height 1.5 meters
Sandstone boulders form the chamber's structure, with a slightly rectangular shape that widens toward the entrance. Stones are layered in four tiers with slanted walls. The floor is paved with river stones. The chamber's roofing consists of three large slabs, while the passage also features river stone paving and three ceiling stones.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (toward rear wall) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (toward entrance) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), sendou-2.jpg|Passage (toward entrance) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), funkyu-3.jpg|Mound and chamber entrance </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Saitobaru Kofun Cluster''' (Special Historic Site since 1952) * Artifacts are displayed at the {{ill|Miyazaki Prefectural Saitobaru Archaeological Museum|ja|宮崎県立西都原考古博物館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Prefectural Saitobaru Archaeological Museum|qq=}}.

== Burial Chamber == The chamber measures: * Total length: 12.4 meters * Main chamber: Length 4.9 meters, width 1.75–2.45 meters, height 2.15 meters * Passage: Length 7.5 meters, width 1.8 meters, height 1.5 meters

The floor is paved with river stones. The chamber's roofing consists of three large slabs, while the passage also features river stone paving and three ceiling stones.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (toward rear wall) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (toward entrance) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), sendou-2.jpg|Passage (toward entrance) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), funkyu-3.jpg|Mound and chamber entrance </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Saitobaru Kofun Cluster''' (Special Historic Site since 1952) * Artifacts are displayed at the {{ill|Miyazaki Prefectural Saitobaru Archaeological Museum|ja|宮崎県立西都原考古博物館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Prefectural Saitobaru Archaeological Museum|qq=}}.

[[ja:鬼の窟古墳 (西都市)]] [[ja:鬼の窟古墳]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito)}} {{wikidata link|Q125960571}} [[Category:History of Saito, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Kofun of Miyazaki prefecture]] [[Category:National historic sites in Miyazaki prefecture]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito)]][[ja:鬼の窟古墳 (西都市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito)]][[ja:鬼の窟古墳 (西都市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鬼の窟古墳 (西都市)]] {{Infobox ancient site}} '''Oni no Iwaya Kofun''' (鬼の窟古墳) or '''Saitobaru No. 206 Kofun''' (西都原206号墳) is a {{ill|circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/circular Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} located in Miyake, {{ill|Saito, Miyazaki|en|Saito, Miyazaki|WD=Q850479|lt=Saito|1=Saito, Miyazaki}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, Japan. It is part of the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|en|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|WD=Q3469314|lt=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|1=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster}}, designated as a [[Special Historic Site of Japan]].
== Overview == {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|en|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|WD=Q3469314|lt=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|1=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster}} is located on a plateau along the southern bank of the Hitotsuse River in central Miyazaki Prefecture. This particular kofun was constructed independently at the center of the cluster. Its name derives from a local legend where a demon, infatuated with {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花開耶姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}}, built the stone chamber overnight. <ref name="説明板">Historical site information board.</ref>
The kofun has been known since the Meiji period, with its first documentation by {{ill|William Gowland|en|William Gowland|WD=Q3568666|lt=William Gowland|1=William Gowland}}. It underwent detailed surveys in 1984 and 1992 and preservation work in 1995.
The mound is circular, measuring 36.4 meters east-west, 33.6 meters north-south, and 7.3 meters high. It has a two-tiered structure with no evidence of {{ill|Fukiishi|en|Fukiishi|WD=Q11621213|lt=fukiishi|1=Fukiishi}} on its surface. Surrounding the mound are an outer embankment and double moats, with the inner moat (内壕) 9.9-11 meters wide, the embankment 5.2 meters high, and the outer moat (外壕) approximately 5 meters wide.
The burial chamber is a {{ill|yokoana-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yokoana-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}, accessible from the south, with an overall length of 12.5 meters. The chamber's construction employs massive sandstone blocks. Artifacts recovered include earrings, jade, iron arrowheads, gilt-bronze harness fittings, and pottery ({{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}}), indicating its construction in the late 6th to early 7th century. Adjacent to the southwest is No. 205 Kofun, believed to be a satellite mound due to overlapping pottery finds.
The kofun is considered the last key burial of a regional leader immediately following the cessation of {{ill|Zenpōkōenfun|en|Zenpōkōenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpōkōenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} construction in Saitobaru. Its similarities with the {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|en|Ishibutai Kofun|WD=Q3816159|lt=Ishibutai Kofun|1=Ishibutai Kofun}} in Nara Prefecture suggest close ties with the Yamato court.
== Excavation History == * 1823 (Bunsei 6): Kodama Sanemitsu documented the chamber in "Hyuga no Kuni Kamiyo Ezu." <ref>{{Harvid|宮崎県史 資料編 考古2|1993}}</ref> * 1872-1888 (Meiji 5-21): William Gowland created schematic diagrams and introduced the kofun in his work on Japanese dolmens. * 1934 (Showa 9): Designated a [[National Historic Site of Japan]] as part of the "Saitobaru Kofun Cluster." * 1952 (Showa 27): Elevated to a {{ill|Special Historic Site|en|Special Historic Site|WD=Q26764449|lt=Special Historic Site|1=Special Historic Site}}. * 1984 (Showa 59): Survey and chamber measurements. * 1995 (Heisei 7): Excavation and preservation work. * Reconstruction work restored the chamber's forecourt and part of its passage.
== Burial Chamber == The chamber measures: * Total length: 12.4 meters * Main chamber: Length 4.9 meters, width 1.75–2.45 meters, height 2.15 meters * Passage: Length 7.5 meters, width 1.8 meters, height 1.5 meters
Sandstone boulders form the chamber's structure, with a slightly rectangular shape that widens toward the entrance. Stones are layered in four tiers with slanted walls. The floor is paved with river stones. The chamber's roofing consists of three large slabs, while the passage also features river stone paving and three ceiling stones.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), genshitsu-1.jpg|Main chamber (toward rear wall) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber (toward entrance) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), sendou-2.jpg|Passage (toward entrance) File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Oni-no-Iwaya Kofun (Saito), funkyu-3.jpg|Mound and chamber entrance </gallery>
== Cultural Properties == * '''Saitobaru Kofun Cluster''' (Special Historic Site since 1952) * Artifacts are displayed at the {{ill|Miyazaki Prefectural Saitobaru Archaeological Museum|ja|宮崎県立西都原考古博物館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Prefectural Saitobaru Archaeological Museum|qq=}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Miyazaki Prefecture History (Archaeology Section, Volume 2), Miyazaki Prefecture, 1993. * "Saitobaru Kofun Cluster Excavation Report," Miyazaki Prefectural Board of Education, 2000.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|en|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|WD=Q3469314|lt=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|1=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster}} * {{ill|Jōshinzuka Kofun|en|Jōshinzuka Kofun|WD=Q11481383|lt=Jōshinzuka Kofun|1=Jōshinzuka Kofun}}
